preFix.title: Replacing a drawer bottom panel
preFix.subtitle: Remove a split or sagging drawer base and fit a new panel in the original plough groove
preFix.excerpt: How to remove a drawer from its carcass, dismantle the drawer box to free the old bottom panel, measure the plough groove for the replacement panel thickness, cut a new plywood or hardboard panel, dry-fit it, glue and pin the drawer box back together, and check the drawer for square before the glue sets.
preFix.sourceNotes: Furniture Restoration and Repair, William Cook. Cabinet-making and Furniture Design, Ernest Joyce. The Workshop, Roy Underhill.

content.text: A split or sagging
drawer base

is one of the most common furniture repairs. If the drawer box is otherwise sound, the base can often be slid out from the back without dismantling the joints. The repair requires cutting a new panel to fit the original
plough groove
: measure the groove carefully before buying replacement material.
Step 1, remove the drawer and assess the joints
Pull the drawer fully out of the carcass. Turn it over and inspect the
drawer base

from underneath. Check whether the back of the drawer box is pinned or screwed through the base into the drawer back. Remove the pin or screw. Attempt to slide the old base out from the back without dismantling the corner joints. If the base slides free, the joints are intact and the drawer box does not need to be taken apart: proceed to Step 3 after measuring the base.
Step 2, dismantle the drawer box if necessary

If the base is swollen or the corner joints are failing, the drawer box must be dismantled. Tap the drawer back off the sides with a mallet and scrap block: work evenly along the joint rather than levering. Remove the old base from the
plough groove
. Clean old glue from all joint surfaces with the chisel or a scraper.
Step 3, measure and cut the replacement panel
Measure the depth of the
plough groove

using the combination square: this determines the required panel thickness. Measure the internal width of the drawer from groove bottom to groove bottom (not the overall width), and the depth from the front groove to the back edge. Cut the replacement
drawer base
panel to these dimensions with the panel saw, then sand all four edges smooth. The panel should slide into the groove without force but with no rattle.
Step 4, reassemble the drawer box
Slide the new base into the
plough grooves
in the front and sides: do not glue the base; it must be free to move with humidity changes. Glue and reassemble the corner joints. Before the glue sets, check the drawer for square by
measuring both diagonals
. If the diagonals are unequal, apply a cramp across the longer diagonal until they match.
Step 5, pin the base and test
Once the glue is set, drive a panel pin through the back edge of the
drawer base

into the bottom of the drawer back to prevent the panel from sliding back out under load. Replace the drawer in the carcass and test it fully loaded. A drawer that binds after reassembly is usually out of square: remove and check the diagonals again.
Variations

Where the original base was solid wood, match the species and cut the panel on the flat grain. A quarter-sawn panel moves less across its width than a flat-sawn one and is less likely to bow. On antique drawers where the base is nailed to the sides rather than sitting in a groove, replace it by removing the nails, cutting a new panel to the same dimensions, and re-nailing with cut-floor brads.

attrs.fix: Reduce the panel width by running each long edge lightly along 120 grit abrasive on a flat surface. Test frequently — remove a small amount at a time. The groove at the front is often narrower than the sides on older hand-cut drawers.
attrs.cause: Panel cut fractionally too wide; sanding did not remove enough material from the edges; groove depth variation between front and sides.
attrs.symptom: Replacement panel will not slide into the groove despite measuring correctly

attrs.fix: Remove the drawer. Check both diagonals with the tape measure. If unequal, soften the glue joints with a heat gun and re-clamp across the longer diagonal. Alternatively, plane the binding corner of the drawer side slightly.
attrs.cause: Drawer box not square after gluing; reassembled drawer is parallelogram-shaped, causing one corner to bind in the carcass opening.

attrs.fix: Replace with a stiffer material — 6 mm birch plywood for wider drawers (over 400 mm). Alternatively, glue a central support strip of hardwood across the underside of the base to reduce the unsupported span.
attrs.cause: Panel material too thin for the span; plywood or hardboard grade inadequate.
attrs.symptom: New base sags under load within weeks of fitting

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
